_note: Authored by RWH — methodology + speed-to-power assumptions + Batch-Zero caveat + queue-context narrative + license-discipline line + featured #4. Voice-gated. Joined to timeline.json by parcel_id.
author: RWH
methodology: The number the field buys on is speed-to-power; everything else prices off it. This estimates it honestly — a disclosed phase model gated on the interconnection queue, not a scraped promise — and shows its own homework.
assumptions_block: Speed-to-power is the critical path: the interconnection long pole (modulated per parcel by live grid quality — proximity to a ≥138 kV substation and the 345 kV backbone) plus the construction tail. Diligence and permitting run in parallel with the queue, so they don't add to the total. The interconnection duration is the dominant uncertainty and is shown as a P50 with a band, never a single promised date.
batch_zero_caveat: ERCOT's Batch Zero (PGRR145) is actively reshaping large-load interconnection — TSP/ILLE deadlines fall in July 2026 — so historical queue durations are an imperfect guide. Queue/COD dates are self-reported and tend to be optimistic. Treat these as directional, re-checked ~July 2026.
queue_context_narrative: I don't show the ERCOT interconnection-queue model: it's license-restricted, so it's held to backend-abstracted use (the 0–100 Grid Connectivity Index) and the public grid overlay uses HIFLD. The queue context here is public — ERCOT Large Load Integration, EIA generation, and public reporting on ERCOT timelines. Knowing what you can and can't republish is part of the job.
variant_note: The headline number models solar-generation interconnection, consistent with the rest of the screen. The DC large-load toggle uses the ERCOT Large Load Interconnection Study (LLIS) path and a longer construction tail — which is why weak-grid parcels can slip past the energization window on the DC view.
featured_narrative: KAR-000004 is the fast-power parcel: on the 345 kV system with transmission across the property, the interconnection long pole is as short as this geography gets, so the speed-to-power lands at the quick end of the band.
That single number is what the deal sheet prices off — it picks the expected row in the time-to-power sensitivity, and the economics follow. Speed-to-power isn't a stat on a card; it's the gate.
